items.q: How do I add a pet?
items.a: Open the Profile tab and tap '+ Add pet' (or the '+' on the Pets list). Fill in the name, species and other details and save.

items.q: How do I keep a medical record?
items.a: Go to the Medical tab: add visits, vaccines and medications manually, or scan a vet document to recognize it automatically. Tap any record to see its details.

items.q: What are Paws and how do I earn them?
items.a: Paws are points you earn for verifiable pet-care actions — a manual medical record, scanning a document, marking a medication as taken, and more. Open 'How to earn Paws' from the Dashboard for the full list.
Ranks reflect your lifetime charity contribution. As your donated Paws grow, your rank and league go up. You can see your current rank and progress in the Profile.

items.q: How do I support a shelter?
items.a: On the Dashboard tap 'Support a shelter' (or open the charity section) and donate your Paws. Donations act as votes that split the monthly charity pool between shelters.

items.q: How do I export a medical record to PDF?
items.a: In the Medical tab, select a pet and tap the share icon in the header. The app builds a PDF (passport, allergies, vaccines, medications, records, weight) and opens the system share sheet.

items.q: How do I turn on notifications?
items.a: In the Profile, switch 'Notifications' on and allow them in the system prompt. You'll get local reminders for upcoming vaccines, appointments and reminders. The bell on the Dashboard opens the notification center.

items.q: How do I change the language or weight units?
items.a: In the Profile → Settings use the language switch (EN/RU) and the units switch (kg/lb). Your choice is saved and applied across the whole app.

items.q: How do I delete my account?
items.a: In the Profile → Settings tap 'Delete Account' and confirm twice. This permanently deletes all your pets, medical records, documents and account data. It cannot be undone.
Your data lives in your private account and is protected by row-level security — only you can access your pets and records. Vet documents are stored in a private bucket.

items.q: Does the AI assistant replace a vet?
items.a: No. The AI assistant gives general guidance only and is not a diagnosis. For health concerns, emergencies or medication decisions, always consult a veterinarian.

knowledge.title: Knowledge base
knowledge.disclaimer: This is general guidance, not a diagnosis. For health concerns, emergencies or medication decisions, always consult a veterinarian.

items.title: Feeding basics
items.body:
Feed a complete, age-appropriate food and keep fresh water available at all times. Stick to a consistent schedule and measure portions to avoid overfeeding.

Introduce any diet change gradually over 5-7 days to avoid stomach upset. Avoid toxic human foods such as chocolate, grapes, raisins, onions, garlic and xylitol.

items.title: Cats: nutrition
items.body:
Cats are obligate carnivores and need animal-protein-based food. Many cats prefer several small meals a day; wet food helps with hydration and supports urinary health.

Don't feed dog food long-term - it lacks taurine, which is essential for cats. Watch weight closely, as indoor cats easily gain excess weight.

items.title: Dogs: nutrition
items.body:
Choose food matched to size and life stage (puppy, adult, senior). Large breeds and puppies have specific needs - ask your vet about the right formula.

Keep treats to about 10% of daily calories. Chocolate, grapes/raisins, onions and xylitol are toxic to dogs - keep them out of reach.

items.title: Vaccination
items.body:
Vaccines protect against serious infectious diseases. Puppies and kittens get a starter series, followed by boosters on a schedule set by your vet.

Keep a record of dates and the next due date. Rabies vaccination is legally required in many regions and is needed for travel.

items.title: Parasite prevention
items.body:
External parasites (fleas, ticks) and internal parasites (worms) are common and preventable. Use vet-recommended preventives year-round or seasonally as advised.

Check the coat and skin after walks, especially in tick season. Deworm on schedule - some worms can affect people too.

items.title: When to see a vet
items.body:
See a vet promptly for: repeated vomiting or diarrhea, refusing food for over a day, lethargy, difficulty breathing, blood in urine or stool, sudden weight loss, or limping that doesn't improve.

When in doubt, call your clinic. Early checks are cheaper and safer than waiting.

items.title: Emergencies - act fast
items.body:
Call a vet immediately for: poisoning, not breathing, choking, seizures, major bleeding, being hit by a car, a bloated hard belly, or collapse.

Keep your clinic's number and the nearest emergency hospital handy. Do not give human medicines unless a vet tells you to.

items.title: Coat & skin care
items.body:
Brush regularly to reduce shedding, prevent mats and spot skin problems early. Frequency depends on coat type - long coats need more.

Bathe only as needed with a pet-safe shampoo; over-bathing dries the skin. Trim nails when they click on the floor.

items.title: Dental care
items.body:
Dental disease is common and painful. Brush teeth with pet toothpaste several times a week - never use human toothpaste.

Offer vet-approved dental chews and watch for bad breath, red gums or tartar. Ask your vet about a professional cleaning if needed.

items.title: Behavior basics
items.body:
Cats need scratching posts, hiding spots and play that mimics hunting. Sudden behavior changes can signal stress or illness.

Dogs thrive on routine, exercise and positive-reinforcement training. Avoid punishment - reward the behavior you want. For persistent issues, consult a vet or behaviorist.

items.title: Seasonal care
items.body:
Heat: never leave a pet in a parked car, walk during cooler hours, and provide shade and water. Watch for heatstroke - heavy panting, drooling, weakness.

Cold: short-haired pets may need a coat; wipe paws after salt and ice. In tick season check the coat daily and keep prevention up to date.

relocation.title: Moving with a pet
relocation.disclaimer: Import rules vary by country and change over time. Always verify the current requirements with official sources and your veterinarian before you travel.

sections.title: Documents
items: Vet passport (international one if travelling abroad)
Pedigree / registration papers (if applicable)
Ownership document or sale contract
Keep paper copies and photos of every document

sections.title: Microchip & ID
items: ISO 11784/11785 compatible microchip implanted
Microchip number matches the passport
ID tag with up-to-date contact details

sections.title: Vaccines & timing
items: Rabies vaccine valid and done in time (often 21+ days before travel)
Core vaccinations up to date
All dates and next-due recorded in the passport

sections.title: Tests & treatments
items: Rabies antibody titre test if the destination requires it
Tapeworm / parasite treatment within the required window before departure
External parasite (tick/flea) treatment before travel

sections.title: Destination rules
items: Check the official import rules of the destination country
Check for quarantine, permits or import licences
Check breed or species restrictions

sections.title: Transport
items: Confirm airline / carrier pet requirements and book the pet slot
Carrier of the correct size, ventilated and labelled
Water and a familiar item; plan feeding around travel

sections.title: Preparing your pet
items: Crate-train and let the pet get used to the carrier
Plan to reduce stress (calming routine, vet advice if needed)
Vet check that the pet is fit to travel

sections.title: On arrival
items: Allow time to acclimatize to the new home
Find and register with a local veterinarian
Complete any local pet registration if required
